text.title: Requirements Validation Failure Modes
text.summary: Common validation failures: rubber-stamp approval, wrong participants, single-technique reliance, UAT confusion, and LLM-specific pitfalls.

antipattern.description: Rubber-stamp approval: stakeholders sign off without reading or without the authority to approve.
antipattern.reason: A sign-off from someone without decision rights is political theatre; when requirements prove wrong, the signer has no accountability and the BA has no defensible baseline.

antipattern.description: Missing key stakeholders: validation session held without users, domain SMEs, or technical leads.
antipattern.reason: Each missing stakeholder type leaves a class of defects undetected — users find usability gaps, SMEs find domain errors, tech leads find feasibility issues.

antipattern.description: Reviewing 200 requirements in one session without splitting by theme or priority.
antipattern.reason: Attention exhausts by requirement 30; the remaining 170 receive rubber-stamp treatment regardless of intent.

antipattern.description: Confusing validation with UAT: validating requirements after delivery rather than before build.
antipattern.reason: UAT validates the built solution; requirements validation validates the requirement before the build. Different artefact, different audience, different defect cost — at UAT it costs a sprint; at requirements validation it costs a session.

rule.statement: Anchor every validation pass to the Business Need and Solution Scope produced in BABOK 5.1/6.1; do not validate requirements without a stated value model.
rule.rationale: Without a value model (KPIs, OKRs, benefit map), validation devolves into stakeholder opinion about what sounds reasonable.

antipattern.description: Value_class=none requirement left in the validated set without removal or re-elicitation.
antipattern.reason: A requirement that traces to no business value is gold-plating; implementing it wastes delivery capacity and may create technical debt with no benefit offset.

antipattern.description: LLM runs a verification checklist (well-formed, unambiguous, testable) and reports "validation complete."
antipattern.reason: Validation specifically asks "does this requirement deliver value?" — the value question is absent from verification; an LLM that conflates them produces a false green signal.

antipattern.description: LLM invents stakeholder confirmations ("Product Owner confirmed requirement is valid") without an evidence anchor.
antipattern.reason: Hallucinated approvals have no accountability chain; when requirements prove wrong, the invented confirmation cannot be traced to any human decision.

antipattern.description: LLM proposes new requirements during validation ("we should also consider X").
antipattern.reason: Scope expansion during validation returns the process to elicitation; mixing the two phases corrupts both the baseline and the validation record.

rule.statement: Use adversarial framing in validation prompts: "list three ways each requirement could fail to deliver value" before scoring; avoid "are these requirements OK?"
rule.rationale: LLMs trend toward yes when asked for approval; adversarial framing surfaces defects that affirmative-framing suppresses.
